title: Spatial intelligence, says Fei-Fei Li, is the next frontier in AI - Fast Company
text:
advertisement

03-24-2026 [MOST INNOVATIVE COMPANIES](https://www.fastcompany.com/section/most-innovative-companies)

# Spatial intelligence is the next frontier of AI, says World Labs’ Fei-Fei Li

AI pioneer Fei-Fei Li and her World Labs team are taking artificial intelligence beyond mere words to achieve spatial intelligence.

SHARE

Facebook LinkedIn Bluesky X Link

ADD ON GOOGLE

[Images: Adobe Stock Stock]

BY Mark Sullivan

Listen to this Article More info

0:00 / 0:00

A growing chorus of experts believe that large language models alone will not lead to the industry’s holy grail: [AI](https://www.fastcompany.com/section/artificial-intelligence "AI") that is generally smarter than humans. Today’s AI systems, which are designed to develop a model of words and their meanings, need to move beyond language to achieve some of AI’s biggest promises. They need to perceive the real world.

“We firmly believe that spatial intelligence is the next frontier of artificial intelligence,” says [Fei-Fei Li](https://www.fastcompany.com/user/fei-fei-li) , the AI pioneer whose work laid the foundation for computer vision systems.

Li’s World Labs, which she cofounded with AI researchers Justin Johnson, Christoph Lassner, and Ben Mildenhall two years ago, is building a form of “world model” capable of processing spatial data and developing a physics-based understanding of three-dimensional environments. The company released its first model, Marble, in November. [Marble](https://marble.worldlabs.ai/) generates highly realistic 3D worlds that can be edited within existing game and video FX development workflows. The initial freemium product can help in designing interactive games, entertainment experiences, and built environments.

But one of the biggest beneficiaries of World Labs’ models might be robotics companies, which currently struggle to prepare robots for real-world utility. “You need a 3D environment that is interactable, that has collisions, physics, and dynamics to train and evaluate robots,” Li says.

ABOUT THE AUTHOR

Mark Sullivan is a San Francisco-based senior writer at _Fast Company_ who focuses on chronicling the advance of artificial intelligence and its effects on business and culture. He’s interviewed luminaries from the emerging space including former Google CEO [Eric Schmidt](https://www.fastcompany.com/91232708/eric-schmidt-youre-not-going-to-succeed-unless-ai-is-checking-ai) , Microsoft’s [Mustafa Suleyman](https://www.fastcompany.com/90959853/mustafa-suleyman-inflection-pi) , and OpenAI’s [Brad Lightcap](https://www.fastcompany.com/90961680/openai-brad-lightcap-interview) [More](https://www.fastcompany.com/user/mark-sullivan)
